segments.text: Hi, I'm Ann Campbell and today I'm going
to talk about the key features of
SonarCube 2025.5.
Let's start with some rules. In Python,
we've added 15 new rules to help you
write good lambdas because writing
efficient, maintainable lambda functions
in Python can be challenging. So, I'm
going to linger lovingly across these
slides so that if you want to pause the
video to take a closer look, you can.
Now, moving on to GitHub actions. We're
adding brand new rules for GitHub
actions to help you detect
vulnerabilities and configuration floods
and prevent supply chain attacks. So,
here are the eight new rules we've added
for GitHub actions. I suspect more will
be coming, but I can't promise anything.
In C++, we've added nine new Misra
rules. This brings our coverage up to
150 out of 179 rules, or 84%.
So, here are the miser C++ 2023 rules
we've added in this version. For
Angular, we've added rules to help you
target the most common problems in code
outside of templates. We have rules for
input and pipes, rules for output, and
rules for life cycle.
And now, let's move on to the demo. I'm
going to show you two features in Sonar
Cube this time. Issue sandboxing and
links in global messages. And I'm going
to start with issue sandboxing because
it's kind of a big deal. So, for years,
we've been hearing that people were
reluctant to take center cube upgrades.
Not because they didn't want the
features in the new version, and not
because they were afraid the new version
might be buggy, but because they were
afraid of what the new version could do
to their quality gates. And that's
because every new version ships updated
analyzers. And those analyzer
improvements can include not just new
rules, but also updated rules that are
smarter and find issues now that they
didn't before. Now, even though we
backdate those rules to take them out of
your new code period, it can still blow
up your quality gate if you've got a
condition on say no blocker bugs in
overall code. And that's why we invented
issue sandboxing. So, let's take a look
at what that looks like. Now, here's a
project that's failing its quality gate,
but it's failing its quality gate
because it's got 10 issues in new code
that were not introduced by the upgrade.
How do I know they were not introduced
by the upgrade? Because issue sandboxing
is turned on in this instance. And I see
in this brand new section of the
homepage, the issue sandbox. So, the new
issues that were introduced by the
upgrade are in the sandbox. And I see
I've got six security issues here. So,
let's take a look at those. The first
thing I notice here is that they've got
a status in sandbox. Now, as the human
user, I can move this issue out of the
sandbox to open, accepted, whatever.
Once I move it out of the sandbox, I
cannot move it back in. Only the
analyzer on the first analysis after the
upgrade can put an issue in the sandbox.
Now, I want to show another project
here. Why is this project failing its
quality gate? because it's got a
reliability issue that was introduced by
an update. So, we're seeing this issue
here because a user moved it out of the
sandbox back into the normal set of
issues. Now, issues in the sandbox are
not included in issue metrics. but
moving it out of the sandbox into an
open status reincluded it into the
normal issue metrics. The quality gate
was recalculated and now we have a
failing quality gate because of that. So
I've got one issue from a sonar cube
update that's in the open issues and
I've got record that I have five issues
from sonar cube updates moved out of the
sandbox into an open status in
maintainability. Let's take a look at
that. So again, here are my 436 issues
in the sandbox. I can filter for that
status anytime I like. And if I move
this issue to open, then my metrics are
updated automatically. If I go back to
the homepage, I see that I now in
overall code have six issues from the
Sonar Cube update. Now, it's worth
looking briefly at the settings to turn
this on. And I want to mention at this
point that this must be turned on before
the first analysis after an update
because again only the first analysis
after an update can put an issue in the
sandbox. If you do the update, run the
analysis and then turn this on, your
issues won't be in the sandbox. So here
at the global level under
administration, general settings, I've
scrolled down to the sandbox feature.
This has been turned on for the whole
instance and we can configure what types
of issues we would like to sandbox. So a
previous administrator turned on
maintainability of all severities and I
am going to turn on security. You see I
get a warning in the UI. So I'm going to
sandbox all security issues and I'm
going to sandbox
let's see info and low reliability
issues. So, I've got the warnings here
in the UI. I really should think about
what I'm doing here. And when I save it,
I get another warning. Yes, I really do
want to do this. And now I can run my
first analysis after the upgrade to get
my sandboxed issues. So, I hope y'all
enjoy using this feature. I know it's
going to be super useful to a whole lot
of people. Now, on the topic of
understanding the upgrade of an impact,
I want to point out one more thing, and
that's that we've added a resource to
the sonarsource.com website to help you
understand what the rule changes were
between the 9.9 LTA and the 2025.1 LTA.
These reports will be coming for other
versions in between, but it's important
to know if you're still considering
whether or not you should take the
update, which yes, you should. This is a
list of all of the rules that were added
or updated between those two LTA
versions. And now jumping back into the
product, I want to show you one more
thing uh which is around it links in
global announcements. So, here I am
again in administration, general
settings. Here at the very top, I've got
the announcement configurations. So, let
me just start by turning this on. Save
that. And I'm going to refresh the page.
And we see that I have a message. Global
messages appear on all pages. Yes, they
do. Now, I'm going to edit this. And I'm
going to add a link. And once I save
this and refresh again, there I have my
link to the community and it works. And
that's what I wanted to show you in the
demo.
Coming back to the slides, I've got just
a few more things I'd like to share with
you. So first, intain analysis has been
updated to support the WPF framework
inputs. That means that we'll recognize
WPF framework UI controls, data
bindings, command parameters as entry
points and sources of untrusted user
input. In JavaScript TypeScript, we've
actually swapped out the TAN analysis
engine for a new engine we've written
from scratch to provide better
detection. In Go, we've added Go 1.25
syntax parsing. In Python, we've added
parallelization of Python analysis. Now,
this has been available by parameter for
a little while now, but now it's on by
default and will automatically look at
your environment, figure out how many
threads make sense, and use that
automatically. If you want fine grain
control of this, that's still available
with analysis parameters, but now it's
on by default for everyone
automatically. And finally, we've added
a global level SCA toggle. Now,
previously it was either on or off for
your entire instance. And if you had the
license for it, it was on. In very big
instances, this was a problem because
people wanted to try it out with a few
projects first and not impose it on
everyone all at once. So now you have
the ability to turn it off at the
instance level so that you can reenable
it at the project level with analysis
parameters.
And that's all I've got for you today.
Thanks for listening. See you next time.
